captions.text: Wow, who would have thought Pokémon Pokopia  would be a big hit? Who could have predicted
that a first-party Nintendo game exclusive  to their hot new console using the Pokémon
license with gameplay influenced by Minecraft,  Animal Crossing, and basically every massively
popular cozy, home-creating game that ever  plowed a parsnip patch would do pretty well
as long as it basically functioned and didn't  spontaneously urinate acid into the eyes of your
three-year-old? He said sarcastically. I mean,  my eldest refuses to go to sleep without her
complete set of plush Eevee evolutions guarding  outside the door against the tickle monster;
let's face it, the Pokémon IP is the closest thing  to a license to print money in this world besides
owning a donut shop next door to a dispensary.  But let's not be too cynical. Pokopia has many
redeeming qualities even by its own merits;  for starters, of all the recent Pokémon games,
it seems to have had the most effort put into  it. And an explanation for that arises swiftly
when we notice it was developed by  Koei Tecmo as well as Game Freak,
so there was someone on the team who  wasn't content to just phone in the
usual bollocks with their feet resting on the  perfumed bum cheeks of a subjugated Mr. Mime.
Pokopia returns us to the Pokémon Mystery  Dungeon mindset of focusing on the Pokémon
themselves and exactly what they get up  to when there aren't human trainers around
ordering them to gouge each others' eyes out.  We play as a Ditto, the shapeshifting Pokémon,
who has taken the form of their absent trainer  because we really miss gouging out the eyes of our
fellow slaves and being forced to live in Kinder  Surprise toy containers. We find ourselves in a
strange post-apocalyptic land that the humans have  mysteriously abandoned, and our task as presented
to us by a lonely hermitic Tangrowth is to rebuild  the shattered land and encourage the scattered
Pokémon to return and set up home. With the stated  intention of somehow encouraging the humans to
come back as well because without the human  trainers around to order them to mutilate each
other and force them to live in balls, the Pokémon  are all puttering around, doing silly things like
be nice to each other and use their powers  for constructive purposes. I guess dudes like
Scyther might feel their knife hands are being  underutilized in a non-combat game; being able
to chop lumber being small recompense for ending  up in hospital every time you have an itchy bum.
There's a rather thought-provoking melancholy  at the heart of Pokopia's story. I remember
distinctly a moment in one of the ruined  city areas after finding a random text log
referencing Vermilion City, when I started to  think, "Wait... am I IN Vermilion City?" And
only then realized that what I had taken for a  large, mostly destroyed building in the distance
was in fact the wreck of the S.S. Anne. So yeah,  we're in post-apocalyptic Kanto. And one gets
a distinct sense, from the way the Pokémon talk  about how much they're looking forward to bringing
the humans back and all the parties and cuddle  sessions and knife fights they're going to have,
that this journey to discover the fate of humanity  is probably going to have a few heart-wrenching
turns. So for those drawn by the sandbox  aspect of this game and only want it so they
can take pictures of their fuckin' Hitmonchan  sitting atop a replica Peruvian step pyramid,
be aware that there's a surprisingly deep  narrative element to all this. You might
be hit by more feels than you expect from the  capers of the amusing little electric shelter
animals. You might have already picked up on  some slightly Minecraft-y vibes from the way
the environments are constructed from regular  tessellating cubes, hence large wrecked cruise
liners not being immediately identifiable, and  those vibes are by no means setting you astray.
You're free to break up and reconstruct the  terrain to your heart's content unless some
of it needs to be a certain way for a cutscene, in  which case the game goes, "You can't do that. No,
I won't explain why. I'm entitled to set  boundaries in this relationship; fuck you."
And the core gameplay involves constructing  miniature biomes to attract specific Pokémon,
clues to which are scattered through the realm.  So you might have to arrange four patches of grass
next to a tree to attract a Pidgey or a tree  and a chair and a length of rope to attract a
Morbidlydepressedicon or whatever. The goal is to  attract as many Pokémon as possible to the region
and make them all comfortable because if they like  you enough, they will give you little presents,
such as three bits of twig or a leaf, and then  look at you like you're supposed to be thrilled.
It also raises the overall quality of the area,  and once that's high enough, you can progress to
the next area, and that threw me the first time  it happened. I mean, consider the cozy game genre
and its luminaries: your Animal Crossing, your  Stardew Valley. The first rule of such games
is that you stay the fuck in one place and make  it really the fuck nice. Felt a bit odd to put
so much work into the starting village and its  residents only to have to go, "See you later,
losers. I'm gonna hang with the cool kids  on the other side of the loading screen."
But then I suppose the narrative emphasis does  necessitate some kind of progression path. On
that note, another warning for the sandbox likers:  you have to get quite a ways through the story to
unlock all the basic abilities and establish  all the mechanics, but to the game's credit,
there's a lot you can figure out just from  exploring, and if you do happen to, say,
stop up the water flooding Meowth's house before  the critical path mission where he asks you to,
there's always dialogue acknowledging it. "Yeah,  the problem's just in he— wait, you already fixed
it? You actually figured out that you can stop  water flowing by putting stuff in front of it?
Wow! And they say state education's cripplingly  underfunded." Pokopia is keeping a lot of cozy
game balls in the air—Pokéballs, if you will. You  can build houses Minecraft-style out of terrain
Duplos, and Pokémon will happily live in them,  but you can also build them Animal Crossing-style:
stake out a plot, bring a pile of resources  and undocumented Pokémon immigrant laborers,
and wait an actual real-world day; it's very  much a "pick your poison" sort of cozy game where
you're invited to engage with its disparate  elements as much or as little as you want.
But there's a sticking point in that annoying  spot right under my left gum where my fingernail
can't quite get to it, and it's this: I've had  difficulty tearing myself away from the cozy,
"just one more day" cycle of your  Stardews and your Animal Tossings,
but I got bored of Pokopia and stopped after  about a week of grudgingly starting it up each
day with a cry of "Gotta at least try to stick  with it," the rejected Pokémon slogan before
"Gotta Catch 'Em All." And what I put that down  to is choice paralysis. There's always so many
things I could be doing at any moment and so  many fucking Pokémon. Impressive though it is
that they all have distinct personalities and  dialogue, they all want to be made comfortable,
and I don't know where to start. Whenever I was  opening the game, thinking, "I bet the beach house
I ordered will be finished constructing  now," the follow-up thought was always,
"Not that it'll appease the ungrateful little  pissants for long." In the final analysis, Pokopia
and I failed to meet each other halfway. It's  trying to do probably too many things at once,
and for my part, I'm not that into Pokémon, and I  think the game expects you to have favorites. You
can select a specific Pokémon to come and live in  your house so you can try your luck with the old
"Oh, how embarrassing; I must have forgotten to  buy a second bed" routine, but I can't get that
attached. Especially not to Morbidlydepressedicon;  boy, that guy's a fucking project.
